content.problemStatement: There are n+1 (0 < n <= 109, indexed 0..n) houses lying on a straight street. One day, they are bombed by an enemy. CodeChef is designing defence systems to protect the street from bombing. Each defense system can protect all houses in some interval, and a house can be protected by more than one system. A defence system can be also moved at any time. By agents, CodeChef knows the bombing schedule of the enemy. Each time they bomb a house and CodeChef wants to know how many systems are protecting that house.
constraints: Sum of m over all test cases ≤250000
All indices used in operations satisfy the bounds described in the statement
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers n and m (0≤n≤10^9, 1≤m≤250000). Then m lines follow, each describing an operation:
- P u v : add a new defence system that protects every house from u to v (0≤u≤v≤n). Systems are indexed by the order of appearance, starting from 1.
- M i d : move the i‑th system by d houses (d may be negative). The system currently covering [u,v] will afterwards cover [u+d,v+d]; it is guaranteed that 0≤u+d≤v+d≤n.
- B x   : the enemy bombs house x (0≤x≤n). For each B operation you must output how many systems currently protect house x.
content.outputFormat: For each bombing operation (B) in the order they appear, output a single line containing the number of defence systems that protect the bombed house.

solution.approach: We need to support three operations on a set of intervals on a line of length up to 10^9: add an interval, move an existing interval, and query how many intervals contain a point. A classic technique for range‑add / point‑query is a Fenwick tree on a difference array: add +1 at the left end of an interval and -1 at (right+1). The prefix sum at position x equals the number of active intervals covering x. Because the coordinate range is huge but the total number of distinct coordinates that ever appear is O(m), we compress all coordinates that can ever be used (every left end, every right+1 end and every query point). We first read all operations while simulating moves to know every coordinate that will appear, store the operations and build a sorted list of unique coordinates. In a second pass we process the operations using a Fenwick tree of size equal to the number of compressed coordinates: • P u v → add +1 at idx(u) and -1 at idx(v+1). • M i d → let the i‑th system currently be [l,r]; remove it by adding -1 at idx(l) and +1 at idx(r+1); compute the new interval [l+d,r+d]; add it by +1 at idx(l+d) and -1 at idx(r+d+1). • B x → answer = prefix sum up to idx(x). All updates and queries are O(log M) where M≤2·m+number of queries, easily fitting the limits. The total time per test case is O(m log m) and the extra memory is O(m).
solution.timeComplexity: O(m log m) per test case (m is the number of operations in the test case).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(m) for storing operations, current intervals and the Fenwick tree.
